items.title: Define the task and metric before collecting data
items.avoid: Gathering a large dataset without stating what decision the model will make or how success is measured.
items.prefer: Write task T and performance measure P first (e.g. "flag spam; optimize precision at 99% recall").
items.why: Clear objectives prevent building models that optimize the wrong business outcome.
items.example: Churn model targets 30-day cancellation, evaluated with PR-AUC—not generic accuracy on balanced dummy labels.

items.title: Split data before any peeking
items.avoid: Tuning features or hyperparameters using test-set results repeatedly.
items.prefer: Train on train, tune on validation, report final numbers once on a locked test set.
items.why: Test leakage inflates reported performance and causes production surprises.
items.example: Normalize using train mean/std only, then apply those stats to val/test rows.

items.title: Start simple, then add complexity
items.avoid: Deploying a large neural network as the first solution on a small tabular problem.
items.prefer: Try logistic regression or a shallow tree baseline; upgrade only when the baseline fails on validation.
items.why: Simple models train faster, debug easier, and set a performance floor you must beat.
items.example: Baselines on 5k loan rows: logistic AUC 0.82; only then try gradient boosting.

items.title: Treat training and inference as separate systems
items.avoid: Running expensive preprocessing or retraining inside the live request path without caching.
items.prefer: Batch-train offline, version artifacts, serve frozen weights for low-latency inference.
items.why: Users need fast predictions; learning from data is a periodic batch job, not per-click retraining.
items.example: Nightly retrain at 02:00 UTC; API loads `model_v14.pkl` for daytime scoring only.
